turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.

Under this Court's attenuation analysis, such evidence is admissible when, as here, the predicate stop was not flagrant but resulted from an objectively reasonable miscalculation. And under Utah's rule, we 짯짯 there would be nothing to stop police officers from 짯짯 from stopping people on the street, articulating something. Terry doesn't take much. Most 짯짯 most officers can articulate some sort of justification, looking for the warrant, and then sending people on their way. So 짯짯 so we believe that deterrence would be very well served by adopting our rule.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. If I could just make three brief points in response. First, to the suggestion that officers make random stops in order to find a warrant to conduct searches of this type. There's actually no evidence of the 짯짯 in this record that that's what happened here, or that it happens more broadly. In fact, I think the opposite is true. If you look at page 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It's routine practice to 짯짯 to run warrant checks. Every stop, legal or illegal, he says it's 짯짯 he runs warrants, on the street or in a car. Meaning, that's what the police officer testified to.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: He 짯짯 he runs them, Justice Sotomayor, for the purposes I think that have been discussed today, for safety rationales and other reasons. But there's no actual evidence that he runs 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So we now have a new rule. We've taken running warrants for traffic stops that we've thought were legitimate because they had to do with highway safety. Now we're saying to police officers, run warrants on any name you get because all you have to do is wave the flag of safety.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: No, Your Honor. That's not what we're saying. We're saying that there is a safety rationale for the warrant check. But beyond that, there's also the important flagrancy safety valve that we've talked about here. And with respect to this particular warrant check on page 101 of the Appendix to our petition, there is actually a finding from the district court that the reason that this officer stopped this particular defendant was on suspicion of drug possession or distribution. It wasn't for something else. So that finding is here. And more broadly, this rule, as we've noted in our papers, is, in fact, the majority rule among the courts that have addressed this issue throughout the country. In the United States, and 짯짯 excuse me, in the Seventh Circuit, the case of United States v. Green was decided in 1997, almost 20 years ago. And that, of course, involves States with a large number of metropolitan areas, Chicago and Milwaukee and Indianapolis. And there's no evidence in this record or before this Court that these sort of random stops in order to check for warrants is happening in those jurisdictions. So I think the actual practice and the way it bears out, and has borne out in 짯짯 in areas where this has been adopted undermines that particular argument. Second, Your Honor, with respect to the question of whether a subjective purpose should come in, responding to Justice Kennedy's question to this 짯짯 to this inquiry. I think if this Court were to do that, it would become an outlier of sorts in the case 짯짯 in this Court's Fourth Amendment jurisprudence. And I think, with respect, if 짯짯 if it remains an objective inquiry and consistent with the rest of this Court's cases, that objective inquiry will capture the flagrant cases. We've cited four cases from four different State courts in our reply brief, in footnote 1 of our reply brief. That's Illinois, Missouri, New Jersey. And Oregon, where the courts that have applied this rule have undertaken the flagrancy inquiry and have, in fact, suppressed evidence because the initial stop was flagrant. Finally, a third point, the 짯짯 as we've noted in our briefs, Your Honor, the 짯짯 the Respondent here has abandoned the Utah Supreme Court's rule that an intervening circumstance must, in fact, be something attributable to the defendant's own free will. We think that's appropriate based on that concession that it would 짯짯 that this Court should 짯짯 should reverse the judgment of the Utah Supreme Court, and leaves the question, of course, of what rule to adopt instead. And we think, Your Honor, this 짯짯 this intervening circumstance here, this arrest on a preexisting warrant that arises from probable cause based on facts completely unrelated to the circumstances and the facts of this stop, is exactly like what happened in Johnson v. Louisiana, which is the case that this Court pointed to in Brown v. Illinois. Where 짯짯 where it adopted and said the intervening circumstance is critical to the attenuation inquiry. It's a straightforward application of that particular test. If there are no further questions.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
